other_observers: Nathan Pieplow and Andy Boyce; Nathan Pieplow first identified the bird
specific_location: east flank of Hoy Mountain, about 420 feet from the Utah state line. Coordinates are N 40 degrees, 42.617 minutes, W 109 degrees, 2.814 minutes
description: the bird was grouse shaped, with a chicken-like body, and significantly smaller (about 2/3 the size) than the the many (45 or more) Blue Grouse we had seen that day. The bird overall appeared more delicate than the Blue Grouse as well. The overall color was a brownish-gray, grayer than the female Blue Grouse we had seen that day, but still with a distinct brown overtone.   The bill was black, and smaller and thinner compared to the head than the bill of a Blue Grouse. The head had a long, thin, "Pyrrhuloxia-like" crest, that was visible at all times, and especially so when the bird was alarmed (as it was during most the observation). There were bold, black ruffs on both sides of the neck, widest at the lower end. The tail was the same brown-gray color as the body, finely barred, with a wide, bold, black terminal band.  I did not see the underparts long enough to notice the patterning or color, and did not notice the patterning on the back or wings.Behaviors: The bird was originally flushed off the ground by Andy Boyce and almost immediately landed on the ground again. For the duration of the observation the bird was on the ground, walking nervously and raising its crest. It flushed after 30 seconds, never to be seen again.Call: none heard. When flushed, the wingbeats could barely be heard, compared to the very loud wingbeats of the many Blue Grouse we had seen that day.Plumage: adult
